articles.primaryKeyword: what to do after arrest kent
articles.metaTitle: Arrested in Kent? What to Do in the First Hour | PSA
articles.metaDescription: Arrested in Kent? The first hour matters most. Your right to free legal advice, what to say, and how to get a representative to the custody suite fast.
articles.h1: The First Hour After a Kent Arrest: What to Do

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: If you have been arrested in Kent, the single most important thing in the first hour is to ask for free, independent legal advice and to wait for that advice before being interviewed. Legal advice at the police station is free to everyone, regardless of income, and is available 24 hours a day.

You do not have to answer questions before you have spoken to a solicitor or accredited police station representative. Asking for legal advice does not make you look guilty and cannot be used against you.

sections.heading: What happens when you arrive in custody
paragraphs: On arrival you will see the custody officer, who must open a custody record, explain why you are being detained, and tell you your rights under PACE Code C: the right to free legal advice, the right to have someone told you have been arrested, and the right to consult the Codes of Practice.

You will be searched, your property logged, and your detention authorised. The custody clock generally allows the police to detain you for up to 24 hours before charge, extendable to 36 hours by a senior officer, and up to 96 hours only with magistrates' authority.

sections.heading: Say these three things
paragraphs: First: 'I want free legal advice.' Second: 'Please tell [name] where I am.' Third: nothing about the allegation until your adviser arrives.
If you are under 18 or a vulnerable adult, an appropriate adult must also be arranged before any interview — say so if it applies to you or your family member.

sections.heading: How to get a representative to a Kent custody suite quickly
paragraphs: When you ask for legal advice, the police contact the Defence Solicitor Call Centre, or your chosen firm directly if you name one. You can name Police Station Agent and we will arrange cover at Kent and Medway custody suites, including out of hours.

Time matters because disclosure, interview, and decisions to charge or release often happen quickly. Having a representative present means your account is recorded properly and your rights are protected from the start.

externalSources: PACE 1984, Code C (Detention, treatment and questioning) — GOV.UK
Right to free legal advice at the police station — GOV.UK 'Being arrested: your rights'
Legal Aid Agency — Advice and assistance at the police station (non-means-tested)
articles.cta: Arrested in Kent right now? Call for free police-station representation, 24/7.

articles.metaTitle: Out-of-Hours Solicitor in Kent Custody (Nights & Weekends)
articles.metaDescription: Need a solicitor at the police station at night or the weekend in Kent? Police station legal advice is free and 24/7. How to get cover fast.
articles.h1: Getting an Out-of-Hours Solicitor in Kent Custody

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Yes — you can get a solicitor or accredited representative at a Kent police station at any time of day or night. The right to free legal advice in custody applies 24 hours a day, including weekends and bank holidays.
The police cannot lawfully interview most detainees who have asked for legal advice until that advice has been provided, so asking protects you even at 3am.

sections.heading: Why nights and weekends are different
paragraphs: Arrests peak in the evenings and at weekends, and custody suites operate around the clock. Duty schemes and firms provide out-of-hours cover precisely because interviews and charging decisions do not wait for office hours.
If you name a firm, the custody officer contacts them; otherwise the Defence Solicitor Call Centre allocates a duty solicitor. Either way the advice is free to you.

sections.heading: How to request cover at a Kent or Medway suite
paragraphs: Tell the custody officer you want free legal advice and, if you wish, name Police Station Agent. We arrange representation across Kent and Medway custody suites, including overnight.
If you are calling on behalf of a family member, give the custody suite location and, if known, the custody reference. We can liaise with the custody officer to confirm attendance.

externalSources: PACE 1984, Code C, sections 6 (right to legal advice) — GOV.UK
Legal Aid Agency — police station advice is free and non-means-tested
Defence Solicitor Call Centre (DSCC) role — Legal Aid Agency guidance

articles.metaTitle: How Much Does a Police Station Solicitor Cost? (Kent)
articles.metaDescription: Police station legal advice is free for everyone. When might you pay privately, what private cover includes, and how Kent fees typically work.
articles.h1: How Much Does a Police Station Solicitor Cost in Kent?

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Legal advice at the police station is free to everyone in England & Wales, whatever your income — this is not the same as legal aid for court, which can be means-tested. So in custody you do not have to pay to be advised and represented in interview.
Some people still choose private representation, for example to instruct a specific firm, for continuity into any later case, or for private (non-legal-aid) work. That is a choice, not a requirement.

sections.heading: Free vs private: what's the difference
paragraphs: Free police-station advice is funded by the Legal Aid Agency and delivered by duty solicitors or your chosen firm. It covers attendance, consultation, and interview representation.

Private representation is paid by you and may be charged at a fixed fee for attendance or an hourly rate. It can be useful where you want a particular solicitor, or where the matter sits outside legal aid scope.

sections.heading: How private Kent fees typically work
paragraphs: Private police-station fees vary by firm and by the time of day, the location of the custody suite, and how long attendance takes. Many firms quote a fixed attendance fee with any additional time charged separately; always ask for the basis of charge in writing before instructing.
Whichever route you choose, the value is the same: a trained representative who reviews disclosure, advises on whether to answer questions, and protects your position.

externalSources: Legal Aid Agency — police station advice is free and non-means-tested (GOV.UK)
GOV.UK — 'Legal aid: eligibility' (court legal aid means testing, by contrast)
SRA Transparency Rules — price information for legal services

articles.metaTitle: PSRAS Exam Format Explained (2026) | PSR Train
articles.metaDescription: How the PSRAS is structured for police station representatives: the written exam, the Critical Incidents Test, the portfolio, and how to prepare.

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: The Police Station Representatives Accreditation Scheme (PSRAS) is the route accredited representatives take to advise clients at the police station. It combines assessed knowledge, an assessed practical exercise (the Critical Incidents Test), and a portfolio of supervised police-station attendances.
Always confirm the current structure, components and pass standard with the SRA and the assessment organisation, because requirements are updated from time to time.

sections.heading: The written/knowledge assessment
paragraphs: The knowledge element tests core law and procedure: PACE and the Codes of Practice (especially Code C and Code D), the caution and the right to silence, detention and the custody clock, vulnerable suspects and appropriate adults, and professional conduct.
Most candidates prepare with timed multiple-choice practice so they can answer accurately under time pressure rather than only recognising the law when they see it.

sections.heading: The Critical Incidents Test (CIT)
paragraphs: The CIT is a practical assessment of how you handle a realistic police-station scenario: identifying the issues, prioritising client consultation, and making defensible decisions on advice. It rewards structured thinking, not memorised quotes.
Practising scenarios out loud — issue-spotting, then a clear decision trail — is the most effective preparation.

sections.heading: The portfolio
paragraphs: Alongside the assessments you build a portfolio evidencing real, supervised police-station attendances. This is firm-led: your supervising solicitor signs off competence against the standards.
Start collecting structured attendance evidence early; good contemporaneous notes make portfolio sign-off far easier.

articles.metaTitle: How to Pass the Critical Incidents Test (CIT) | PSR Train
articles.metaDescription: A structured method for the PSRAS Critical Incidents Test: spot the issues, prioritise consultation, and give a defensible decision trail.
articles.h1: How to Pass the Critical Incidents Test (CIT)

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: The CIT rewards a repeatable method, not recall. Read the scenario, identify the legal and welfare issues, prioritise private consultation with the client, then state a clear, justified decision on advice.
Examiners want to see safe, structured reasoning under pressure — the same thing a real custody case demands.

sections.heading: A four-step framework
paragraphs: 1) Issues: list the Code C points (detention basis, custody clock, vulnerability/appropriate adult, fitness to interview). 2) Disclosure: what do you know, and what is missing? 3) Consultation: what advice follows from the disclosure and the client's instructions? 4) Decision: answer, no comment, or prepared statement — and why.
Saying the framework aloud keeps you calm and makes your reasoning visible to the assessor.

sections.heading: Common mistakes that cost marks
paragraphs: Jumping to 'no comment' without reasoning; ignoring welfare and appropriate-adult triggers; failing to note what disclosure is missing; and giving advice that does not match the client's instructions.
Practising several scenarios end-to-end fixes most of these before the assessment.

externalSources: SRA — PSRAS / Critical Incidents Test assessment criteria
PACE 1984 Code C — detention and questioning, GOV.UK
Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994, ss.34–38 (adverse inferences)
articles.cta: Drill realistic CIT scenarios in the Critical Incidents module.

articles.metaTitle: Free PSRAS Practice Questions (Sample Set) | PSR Train
articles.metaDescription: Try a free set of PSRAS-style practice questions covering PACE Code C, the caution, detention and vulnerable suspects — with worked explanations.

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Practising under timed conditions is the fastest way to lift a PSRAS score, because the knowledge assessment is as much about speed and precision as recall. Start with a short sample set, then review every answer by Code reference.
Below is a sample of the kinds of questions you will face; the full bank and timed mocks are in the course.

sections.heading: Sample topics covered
paragraphs: PACE Code C: detention authorisation and the custody clock; the caution and the right to silence; rest periods and interview breaks; appropriate adults and vulnerable suspects; and the basics of Code D identification.
Each question should be reviewed by the underlying Code provision, not just the correct letter — that is what builds durable knowledge.

sections.heading: How to use the sample
paragraphs: Sit the sample untimed first to learn, then timed to build speed. Log your accuracy by topic so you know where to revise next.
Enter your email to get the free sample set and study tips, then move to the full timed bank when you are ready.

articles.metaTitle: Police Station Attendance Note Template (Free + Software)
articles.metaDescription: What a police station attendance note must contain, a free template structure, and how Custody Note keeps notes LAA-audit ready.

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: A good police station attendance note records identity and references, the reason for attendance, disclosure given by police, welfare and vulnerability issues, the advice given and instructions received, the interview, the outcome, and next steps.
Structure matters: a consistent template makes notes faster to write, easier to bill under the Legal Aid Agency contract, and more defensible if a file is audited.

sections.heading: Template structure
paragraphs: Header: client name, DOB, custody reference, station, dates and times. Disclosure: what the officer told you, and what was missing. Consultation: advice given and the client's instructions. Interview: who attended, key questions, interventions. Outcome: charge, bail, RUI or NFA, and any conditions.
Finish with time recording aligned to your firm's billing fields so the claim writes itself.

sections.heading: Why digital templates win
paragraphs: A digital template enforces the structure, prompts for required fields, and produces a clean PDF for the file. It also works offline — essential where station Wi-Fi is poor.
Custody Note provides exactly this: structured, offline-first attendance notes with LAA-oriented fields and PDF export.

articles.metaTitle: LAA Audit-Proof Attendance Notes: A Checklist
articles.metaDescription: Why attendance notes fail Legal Aid Agency audits and a practical checklist to make your police station files defensible.
articles.h1: LAA Audit-Proof Attendance Notes: A Checklist

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Files most often fail audit because of gaps the practitioner could not later fill: missing times, no record of disclosure, unclear advice, or time claimed that the note does not support. The fix is contemporaneous, structured recording.
If the note shows what you did, when, and why, the claim is defensible.

sections.heading: The checklist
paragraphs: Times for each stage; the custody reference and DSCC reference where relevant; a disclosure summary; welfare and appropriate-adult considerations; the advice given and the client's instructions; the interview record; the outcome; and time recording that matches the narrative.
Each item maps to a billing field — so good notes and good claims are the same task done once.

sections.heading: Build it into your workflow
paragraphs: Capture as you go rather than reconstructing later. A template that prompts for each field prevents the gaps auditors look for.
Custody Note enforces this structure and exports a clean PDF for the file, helping keep claims audit-ready.

externalSources: Legal Aid Agency — Standard Crime Contract Specification (police station work)
Legal Aid Agency — Contract Compliance / audit guidance (GOV.UK)
SRA Standards and Regulations — record keeping

articles.metaTitle: Handwritten vs Digital Custody Notes: Cost & Risk
articles.metaDescription: The real cost and risk of handwritten police station notes versus structured digital attendance notes — speed, legibility, and audit defensibility.

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Handwritten notes are flexible but carry hidden costs: re-typing time, legibility disputes, missed fields, and weaker audit trails. Structured digital notes reduce all four while remaining usable offline.
The question is not paper vs screen but structure vs no structure.

sections.heading: Where handwriting costs you
paragraphs: Time: notes often have to be re-keyed back at the office. Consistency: free-form pages miss required fields. Risk: illegible or incomplete notes are harder to defend on audit or in later proceedings.
These costs are invisible until a file is challenged.

sections.heading: What good digital notes add
paragraphs: A consistent template, prompts for required fields, instant PDF export, and offline-first operation so poor station Wi-Fi is never a blocker.
Custody Note is built for this: encrypted, offline-first attendance notes with LAA-oriented fields.

PACE 1984 Code C — custody record context, GOV.UK
SRA — file management and record-keeping obligations

articles.primaryKeyword: how to become police station representative
articles.metaTitle: How to Become a Police Station Representative (2026)
articles.metaDescription: The 2026 route to becoming an accredited police station representative: PSRAS, supervised attendances, the portfolio, and getting your first instructions.
articles.h1: How to Become a Police Station Representative (2026)

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: To advise clients at the police station you must be accredited through the Police Station Representatives Accreditation Scheme (PSRAS): pass the assessed knowledge element and the Critical Incidents Test, and complete a portfolio of supervised attendances signed off by a solicitor.
You work under a firm's supervision; accreditation is portable once achieved.

sections.heading: The route, step by step
paragraphs: 1) Get linked to a firm that can supervise you and provide attendances. 2) Build underpinning knowledge of PACE and the Codes. 3) Sit the assessments. 4) Complete supervised attendances and portfolio sign-off. Confirm the current components with the SRA and the assessment organisation.
Many candidates prepare the knowledge and CIT elements with a dedicated question bank and scenario practice.

sections.heading: Getting your first instructions
paragraphs: Once accredited, freelance reps find work through firm relationships and directories. A clear profile — areas covered, availability, and accreditation status — helps firms instruct you with confidence.
Listing in a reputable directory makes you discoverable to firms needing cover.

externalSources: SRA — Police Station Representatives Accreditation Scheme (PSRAS)
Law Society — becoming a police station representative
PACE 1984 Codes of Practice C and D — GOV.UK
articles.cta: Accredited or training? List in the PSR UK directory and get found by firms.

articles.metaDescription: How freelance police station representatives set rates in 2026: per-attendance vs day-rate, what drives price, and how to quote firms with confidence.
articles.h1: Setting Your Freelance Rep Day-Rate in 2026

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: Most freelance police station reps are paid per attendance rather than a flat day-rate, because firms claim police station work under fixed Legal Aid Agency fees. Your rate should reflect travel, time of day, complexity, and reliability.
Be transparent: agree the basis of charge with each firm in writing before you accept cover.

sections.heading: What drives your rate
paragraphs: Geography and travel time; out-of-hours and weekend premiums; the volume and reliability you offer a firm; and your accreditation and experience. Firms value reps who turn up promptly and produce clean attendance notes.
Reliability and good notes let you command the upper end of the range and win repeat instructions.

sections.heading: Quoting firms with confidence
paragraphs: Offer a clear schedule: per-attendance fee, any travel or unsocial-hours uplift, and your turnaround for the attendance note. Keep it simple so firms can say yes quickly.
A professional profile and directory listing make it easier for firms to find and trust your rate.

articles.metaTitle: Building a Firm Panel of Freelance Reps (Guide)
articles.metaDescription: For criminal defence firms: how to build a reliable panel of freelance police station representatives for cover, including vetting and conflict checks.
articles.h1: Building a Firm Panel of Freelance Reps

sections.heading: Answer first
paragraphs: A reliable panel of accredited freelance reps gives a firm resilient out-of-hours and overflow cover without permanent headcount. Build it on three things: verified accreditation, clear geography/availability, and consistent note quality.
Set expectations once, in writing, and the panel runs itself.

sections.heading: Vetting and onboarding
paragraphs: Confirm PSRAS accreditation and indemnity position, agree the basis of charge, and set your attendance-note standard and turnaround. A short onboarding pack saves repeated explanation.
Keep records of who covers which custody suites so allocation is fast at 2am.

sections.heading: Conflict checks and quality
paragraphs: Require a conflict check before each instruction and a defined note standard for every attendance, so files stay audit-ready regardless of who attended.
Sourcing reps through a reputable directory speeds up panel-building and gives you verified profiles.

externalSources: SRA Standards and Regulations — supervision, competence and conflicts
Legal Aid Agency — Standard Crime Contract (use of agents/reps)
Law Society — managing agency and locum arrangements
